planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; chocolate habanero requires warm germination temps (80–90°F) — use a heat mat for reliable sprouting; transplant outdoors only after soil is consistently warm.

directions: Start seeds on a heat mat at 85°F for reliable germination 10–21 days; transplant after last frost when nighttime temperatures stay above 60°F. Chocolate Habanero (also called Congo Black or Dark Habanero) is a Capsicum chinense variety that ripens to a deep chocolate brown color rather than the typical orange, with heat levels of 300,000–400,000 Scoville units — significantly hotter than standard orange habaneros and with a distinctive earthy, smoky-sweet flavor that many hot pepper enthusiasts consider more complex and interesting. Originally from Jamaica and Trinidad, it is prized by hot sauce makers for its unique color and flavor profile; the pods are slightly larger than standard habaneros, with thick walls and a rich, fruity aroma. Grow in full sun with warm nights for the best fruit set; perennial in zone 9–12, often producing for multiple years as a shrubby plant.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University

planting.note: Start on a heat mat 8–10 weeks before last frost; Red Savina is a selected strain of habanero developed by Frank Garcia of GNS Spices that held the Guinness World Record for hottest pepper from 1994 to 2006, reaching 350,000–580,000 Scoville units — significantly hotter than standard habaneros.

directions: Germinate on a heat mat at 85°F and grow under strong grow lights until transplanting outdoors after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; Red Savina grows as a compact, bushy plant and does best with consistent moisture and fertilization in full sun. Red Savina was developed by Frank Garcia in Walnut, California as a selected sport of the standard orange habanero and registered with the US Plant Variety Protection office; from 1994 until 2006 it held the Guinness World Record for hottest pepper at 350,000–580,000 Scoville Heat Units — since surpassed by even hotter superhot varieties but still among the most intensely hot widely available peppers. The fruits are classic habanero-shaped, medium-sized, bright red at maturity, with the characteristic fruity, apricot-like habanero aroma and overwhelming heat. Often grown by heat enthusiasts who want a genuinely hot habanero with well-established provenance and consistent heat levels.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University
Dave DeWitt's The Chile Pepper Encyclopedia

planting.note: Start on a heat mat 8–10 weeks before last frost; Fatalii originates from central and southern Africa, where it has been grown for generations as a culinary pepper; it ripens to bright yellow (sometimes orange or red) with a distinctive citrus-like fruity aroma combined with extreme heat of 125,000–400,000 Scoville units.

directions: Germinate seeds at 85°F on a heat mat; transplant after last frost into rich, well-drained soil in full sun with regular moisture. Fatalii is a Capsicum chinense variety originating in central Africa — most likely from the Central African Republic or surrounding region — that has been grown by chileheads and pepper enthusiasts for decades as an outstanding 'extreme fruity heat' pepper; at 125,000–400,000 SHU it surpasses most habaneros in heat while delivering a distinctive, intensely citrusy, almost lemon-lime fruity aroma that makes it popular in hot sauces and fruity salsas where both extreme heat and complex flavor are desired. The fruits are elongated, pointed, and wrinkled at maturity, ripening to a brilliant golden yellow. Highly productive in warm climates; the plants are vigorous and tall for a Capsicum chinense, often reaching 3–3.5 feet.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University
Dave DeWitt's The Chile Pepper Encyclopedia

planting.note: Start on a heat mat 10–12 weeks before last frost; Trinidad Moruga Scorpion is one of the hottest naturally occurring chiles in the world, measured at 1.2–2.0 million Scoville units — handle with gloves and avoid touching eyes or face; it was certified by the Chile Pepper Institute as the world's hottest pepper in 2012.

directions: Germinate at 85–90°F on a heat mat — germination can take 3–4 weeks for superhot varieties; grow under bright lights and transplant only when nighttime temperatures are consistently above 60°F. Trinidad Moruga Scorpion originated in the Moruga district of Trinidad and was certified by the Chile Pepper Institute at New Mexico State University as the world's hottest pepper in 2012, with an average heat of 1.2 million SHU and individual samples reaching 2 million SHU — making it one of the hottest naturally occurring chile varieties ever measured. The fruits are round to elongated with a characteristic tail or 'stinger,' ripening to red (sometimes yellow or chocolate depending on the phenotype), with intense fruity tropical flavor beneath the extraordinary heat. Widely grown by superhot enthusiasts for hot sauce and extreme culinary use; handle seeds and fruits with nitrile gloves and eye protection.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University

planting.note: Start on a heat mat 10–12 weeks before last frost; 7 Pot Primo is a Louisiana-developed superhot cross between a Naga Morich and a Trinidad 7 Pot pepper, measuring 1.0–1.5 million Scoville units, with a distinctive elongated tail; handle with gloves and treat as an extreme culinary novelty — a single pod flavors a large batch of hot sauce.

directions: Germinate at 85°F on a heat mat for 2–4 weeks; grow under strong lights before transplanting outdoors after all frost risk has passed. 7 Pot Primo was developed by Louisiana hot pepper grower Troy Primeaux (the 'Primo' in the name) as a cross between a Naga Morich and a Trinidad 7 Pot variety; the name '7 Pot' refers to the traditional Trinidadian claim that one pod is hot enough to season 7 pots of food. At 1.0–1.5 million SHU it is among the most intensely hot peppers available to home growers, with a distinctive gnarled, elongated 'tail' that distinguishes it from the rounder Trinidad 7 Pot types. The flavor beneath the heat is described as fruity, earthy, and robust; used in extremely small quantities in hot sauces, fermented hot pepper mash, and as a smoking or drying chile for superhot spice blends.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University
Superhot Pepper Forum — The Hottest Pepper community records
